speaker: interviewer
text: Fira, when you eventually met up with your brother-in-law and sister, how was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was really-- you know, I felt that I was not-- not alone. So we were-- then I start to look for work. And it just happen-- also, I want to mention when we were working together with my sister, she-- she developed a terrible cough--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Tomsk still.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tomsk. And the work was quite very far up the hill. And at that time, if you are late for work, they take a-- 20%, like, from your wages. And so she developed such a cough on the way to work that she-- and here the sirens started that-- to-- already to be at work. And I couldn't leave her. She was blue. And I couldn't leave her. But she told me, you run, because they wouldn't believe us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came to work, and I was worried what happened to her. Eventually, maybe half an hour later, she came. She came there also because we-- we were afraid they will-- it was a big prosecution, you know? Did-- did--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why when I-- my brother-in-law send me-- sent for me, I ran away from work. They didn't know what happened to me. And I was not legal. I didn't had a ticket. He paid the woman which was the conductor of that wagon. She-- one day she came to work, and she gave me a letter written in Yiddish that-- to-- to go to this address to this woman and to talk over. She will tell me all-- what it is about. He was mobilizing, but he had a good job in the army. He was working in the-- where they repair shoes. He-- it wasn't his profession, but it was a big privilege in Siberia to be near shoes or near bread. So he went and-- and promised her that he will repair for her children, for her, and he will make her a-- a new pair of boots, so that she could bring me-- smuggle me over. So after work-- and-- In this letter, he said not to mention to anyone. After work, I went to this woman. And she told me it went-- this train went twice a week only. And she-- she was afraid too. So she said, maybe you will change your mind. Maybe next time. I said, no. I-- if I have opportunity-- I was-- you know, I-- this was my worstest year. That was 1942. I was hungry. I-- I fainted many times. So they used to give me-- it was always a-- a-- a stove with boiled water. So that's was it. And, you know, and I was frozen. I didn't have clothes. I used-- I had quilted a few things to have [INAUDIBLE] on my-- I tied up on  my knees, because the frost was unbearable. I didn't have the right clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I thought, if I have opportunity, I have to go now. So I went back to these two women. I told them that I am going. And it worked, [? most ?] probably they thought if they would caught me, I would be-- I would spend in jail. So-- and I came to the station with-- and she was a lot of-- you know, she pushed me in-- in--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the conductor has a little-- little room there where they'd been sitting because it takes about 14 hours or so to-- to travel. And she put me on a bank next to her. Then it was a control. When it was a control, I was half asleep. She-- they-- she walked-- they came in, and she walks with-- with them to check tickets and all the papers. It was like-- like a big thing to travel. She caught my leg. I jumped down. And she dragged me behind. They were in front, and she was behind them, and she dragged me. And then we came into the last cubicle, where it was it was some people sitting. It was dark. And she pushed me in there. She said, oh, these people, are traveling already along-- all the way with me. And that's when they went out. The second time came was during the day, so as soon as they came in, she put me in-- into where was the boiler to keep warm for the-- And it was a small cubicle. I was in my wool coat. And I was standing pressed to the wall. And this heat there-- the furnace, you know, the fire there-- for me, I thought it last hours. Maybe it didn't so long. And I was all dripping wet. And that's how she-- she brought me over--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived, she knew the address. She took me to my sister and brother. She knocked at the door. She said, I couldn't bring her. And then she opens the door. Could you imagine? So from-- and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name? Did she--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. And then-- so I was happy. And then I start to look for work, but being hungry for such a long time, I was looking only for work where food is. Eventually, it was a big thing, you know. I came to-- to this factory. The whole city in Stalinsk Kuznetsky was this factory. Everything-- they had their shops, their fields, their-- their everything. It was a metallurgy city and a university. It was metallurgy from all of Russia. Whoever started metallurgy came to this town.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to look for a job, and I came into this [INAUDIBLE]. So you have to cross three secretaries still. He was a big shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came in. I had the nice fair head and nicely dress. This is what I had. And you know, I look different. When I came in, he starts to ask me where I come from. I told him from Taganrog.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened, he had a brother working in a big factory, also such a big post in Taganrog. And he asked me, and I was afraid. Should I tell him what I know? Or I-- I could be a spy. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, he told-- he asked me questions. As soon I came in, he phoned the secretary that today he's not available anymore. And he-- I was sitting a whole afternoon with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somehow I was-- I was lucky. And he want to give me a job in the-- where the-- the engineering-- And I thought they-- they get such-- in offices they had 400 gram bread, and that was practically as small slice of bread on the ration card. And thought, what-- what will I do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, look, they are intelligent people, engineers. And you-- you-- I still said, I want to work in a-- in a canteen. He said, you know, our workers are very rough, and you are not suited for this. And I-- he still want me to. And I still say-- so eventually, he said, all right, I will try to do something for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he sent me to the city. From-- it was director which also provides the jobs. And-- and he gave me a note, and this to whom it's concerned, he made a-- he-- a line. I didn't know what it did. When I-- it was such a long queue, people waiting to-- to get jobs.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was only for the factory, but this was for other places. They had shops. They had the university canteen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so when I came in, she screamed at me, how dare I to-- to underline her name, you know, in front of others? That was only a pretend. And then she gave me-- she sent me to the university to work as a cashier. I learned-- you know, how you call-- the wooden-- Abacus.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I learned there. And there, at least I was near food. I wasn't hungry anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fira, could you speak Russian by now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, not-- not-- you know, I understand everything. But I went there in Taganrog, the first year. I finished seventh grade, because I want to study to be a [? lab rat ?] to work in the laboratory. So I-- I knew perfect. I was so perfect in writing, reading, and-- you know, that sometimes when I came between Russians, I felt so bad that they think that I am one-- one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, Fira, in that time, were you aware of what was happening to-- in-- in Poland and in the rest of Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. Not-- you know, old days, when I was in such a despair, when I was really lonely and-- and hungry and cold, I thought, oh, I will come home, and I will tell my mother what I went through, you know? Not-- not a thing-- we didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and did you experience any anti-Semitism in that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. No. My best friend, I-- at that time-- you know, I was young. I never had boyfriends. I-- I was very strict at home. And so here, I have students. We went out. We-- we went dancing. They were-- I had really good-- good friends between the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long-- how long were you in Stalinsk Kuznetsky?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was there from 1940-- the end of 1942-- that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet your husband there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We met-- matter of fact, he was blond with blue eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Misha-- there, they called him Misha. And it was summer in-- in Stalinsk. And there they have in summer a big-- how you say, they made for dancing in the park and-- a platform. And it was playing the university student-- about 20 people-- a band-- beautiful band. And we used a come there. You know, we met a lot of our young people, Polish people. And I met one-- I came with-- with-- you never came with anybody. You meet there. You meet there. And later we had to go home. This fellow-- I don't-- [? Mietek, ?] I think his name was-- he asked me-- he wasn't a brunette. And, you know, I spoke to him, and sometimes I mixed in a Jewish word or something. And when we had to go home, he asked my husband, [LAUGHS] could he take me, because it wasn't-- it was snow up to here. Maybe he-- he goes in my direction that he will take me home. On the way home, we start to speak. He ask where I come from. And I said from Chelm. He said, he comes from Krakow. And I was so annoyed. I thought of this [? Mietek, ?] look at him. I came-- I came with him. He gave me another Jewish fellow. And I am-- While we were talking, he knowing that this [? Mietek ?] is not Jewish, so he thought that I-- I didn't look Jewish either. So he thought that I'm not Jewish. That's why he asked where I come from, who is [? here. ?] And then we discover we are both Jewish. And we start to go out together. We-- we worked shifts. He was working like from 7:00-- three shifts from 7:00 till 4:00 one week, next week from 4:00 to 12:00, and the third week from 12:00 to 7:00 in the morning. So whenever he worked till 4:00, we met. And we-- we went out together. Then he proposed to me. And we married-- we knew each other only five months, and because New Year's Eve is the day we both had this evening free, so he suggested we get married that evening, because we will maybe celebrate together.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had-- and I had a huppah there. It was an older Jewish man, which had a huppah, because he-- actually in Russia, you don't need to register. And I thought how could-- I will come home something to register without a huppah. I still felt it should be. So I had a huppah. And-- Did you-- did you have a party? How was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: A party-- you know, I was so friendly where I worked in this canteen that they'd been stealing for-- for me meat and-- and beer. I didn't do nothing. The-- the staff and from the-- the manager from where the storeroom-- the-- beer because the students want to celebrate. So they had a-- each two-liter beer and a-- and a dinner that came. You know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So instead beer-- so they took it out beer and poured water there to have the amount. And they-- they were very, very-- they liked me there. I was-- they were very friendly to me. You said-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we had also-- you know, there, you come-- if you-- you have to know somebody to get something. So we had a-- a room. It was like a kitchen and a room. So the room was the Russian woman, and we had like the kitchen. That was-- we moved in.

speaker: interviewer
text: You--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't had a-- I didn't had nothing, not even a proper dress. That's how-- that's how when my daughter married, I put everything into it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fira, just to explain, you said that your husband was called Misha because it was Russia. How did you get the name Fira?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in Russia, Esther is Esfir. And short is Fira, and that's how I became Fira. We-- everybody knows me. I dropped the Esther. They don't know. It took six years, you know, to be under that name, and that's how I stayed with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fira, tell me, when did you realize that the war in Europe-- or in the rest of Europe-- was-- was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: When it was over-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we did-- I had already my daughter. She was five months old. It was in May.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born exactly a year after I got to my first anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1st of January--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945. And I-- I got married a year before. And so my husband went to work. What it was-- they-- it wasn't any radios. We had only microphones. And they always-- you could hear what-- what was given-- not a whole day, sometimes some. And mostly at night, they used to call out with this monotonous voice, you know, like, listen. Listen, you know? And-- and you wake up to listen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Russian were advancing, in every town, they announced which was taken. Once they announced Chelm was the first Polish city what-- what they took over. Could you imagine that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was also this first government established in-- in Chelm. And after maybe two weeks, they moved to Lublin and then to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Chelm-- while I was working in-- in the canteen, it was professors. They used to come for dinner. It was a map hanging, and knowing this-- you know, my geography, they used to discuss with me to show which-- which city which is where, especially on the Ukraine side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when they took Chelm, I was-- I didn't-- I was still-- I was pregnant. And to be pregnant, they had you-- you had all this-- eight months pregnant, I didn't have not one day free, not-- because they say we had to replace like a soldier on the front-- we on the back. But I got the last month. I-- they allowed 30 days before. And I just got it free. When-- and I went to visit my sister. My sister didn't work. She had two children. And when I went to my sister came and letter from my cousin. He-- they were three brothers on the front. One got killed, and this one-- the oldest one-- he was-- entered Chelm with the-- the army. And he went to look for-- for family. For--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meyer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meyer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meyer Appelbaum. That was my cousin's husband. And he sent a letter to my sister, and she didn't want to-- to read in front of me. I was the last month pregnant. And I said, you can-- [CRYING] That's when he-- he said [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no longer they lived. [CRYING] But what it was when my daughter was born, I-- you know, they say in Jewish you can't name if you're not sure. So I didn't know if my mother was [INAUDIBLE] so I called her Ann [INAUDIBLE]. It will be like similar. [CRYING] And then-- so we-- then we start to find out things. And also it start already in Russia-- Russian towns. And they-- they-- you couldn't get a paper, a newspaper, so the news will come a [INAUDIBLE], like a political thing and read the news, and telling all rich city what-- what happened. And when I found my brother, before I got married, the city, Taganrog, was taken over by the Russians. And so I wrote a letter to this woman where we lived. And she received a letter from my brother, and she sent me his address that-- where he is, because all these four I didn't know-- I didn't believe he's alive. And that was the day before I-- I-- the day I got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she wrote in this letter that we were lucky that we escaped, otherwise we-- we would be on-- on the Babi Yar. Did you know? Yevtushenko wrote about-- that we would be on the Babi Yar that was near. [SIGHS] Yes, and-- so-- Fira, when did you decide to leave Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- about the day of the war, when it end-- my husband went to work, and suddenly-- it was May, but in Siberia, the snow was still up to here and dry. And so I notice through the window it-- after maybe an hour or so, he-- he is coming back. And I thought, something must happen to him. I didn't-- we didn't believe. And he came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said-- I said, what are you home? Said-- had something happened? He said, Fira, the war is over. Could you imagine?

speaker: interviewee
text: We packed my daughter in a sled. We went to this big parade. I think it was the fifth of May. And we went to the parade. It was-- but for us, you know. And then, 1940-- '45, they start to do the repatriation of Polish people, could go back. So we registered, and it took us to-- till 1946. We went also-- it took us four weeks from-- already [? end ?] was a year-- a year and three months. So we on the way back, we came to the first Polish city. The train stopped before we crossed the-- the border. So it was Przemsyl. And it was traveling with us a-- a Polack, and it was a Sunday.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we became in, it was morning. We-- we opened the-- the, you know, doors, and the church bells were ringing. And he-- he-- he was crying, you know? So and for us, also we came out from the train. The train stopped, and Przemsyl was already a Jewish community. They start to tell us stories. Because we didn't know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, we came to-- to Tarnow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Tarnow, the train stopped. It was maybe 50 wagons. Also on the way, you know, it was from other-- from Siberia, from other cities coming. And so people used to come out and-- and find and ask. People used to-- lost each other, you know. A lot of [INAUDIBLE] find-- it was-- it was really-- [SIGHS] And then we came to Tarnow. And my husband knew his sister came back from Auschwitz. We-- a matter of fact, being in Siberia, Krakow wasn't taken by then, but he wrote a letter, and the letter arrived-- and the letter arrived-- you know, in the meantime, Krakow was free. And she sent us-- we-- also. That's when we found out that she survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she came-- he left Tarnow and left me with the baby and my sister on the train, and he went to Krakow. And the next day, he came over with her. She said, where will you go with a little baby? You don't know where you're going. And so we went to Krakow. Then-- We'll stop here.
